---
title: "Blades in the Dark"
type: exemplar
medium: tabletop
year: 2017
designer: "John Harper (One Seven Design)"
mechanisms:
  - ../articles/systems-mechanics/mechanical-resolution-types.qmd
  - ../articles/systems-mechanics/progress-clocks-and-faction-state.qmd
  - ../articles/systems-mechanics/playbook-design.qmd
  - ../articles/systems-mechanics/prep-economy.qmd
  - ../articles/story-character-voice/heist-and-caper-patterns.qmd
  - ../articles/players-social-dynamics/faction-design-and-asymmetric-objectives.qmd
provenance: "corpus"
---

*Profile: a crew of scoundrels in a haunted industrial city, run by an engine that turns every dice roll into a small negotiation over danger and reward.*

Blades in the Dark is the rare design that is quoted more often than it is played. A table that has never touched Doskvol still reaches for its progress clocks, still talks about a roll's *position* and *effect*, still lets a player rewind the fiction to explain a precaution they never declared. John Harper published it in 2017 through Evil Hat, after a Kickstarter that funded its modest goal in a single day and an extended public playtest. [corpus] What shipped was a campaign engine for criminal crews that has since been borrowed from, piece by piece, across the hobby.

It earns a place in this gazetteer because so many of its parts travel. Most exemplars here illustrate one or two mechanisms cleanly. Blades illustrates half a dozen, and several of them have escaped the game entirely to become common vocabulary. Reading it is a fast way to see how a handful of small, sharp rules can compose into a living world.

## The design profile

The game is set in Doskvol, a walled port city on a world whose sun has died, where the dead rise as ghosts unless held back by electroplasmic barriers. [corpus] The setting is not backdrop. The dead sun produces the ghost economy, which produces the specific crimes the crew commits and the specific dangers beyond the walls, so the fiction and the mechanics are wound together rather than laid side by side. [synthesis]

Underneath that fiction sits a deliberately blunt resolution core wrapped in a set of framing rules that carry the real weight. The dice stay simple, and the judgement happens before any of them are thrown.

::: {.callout-note}
### The Signature

Before any roll, the table fixes two dials. **Position** says how exposed you are if it goes wrong: controlled, risky, or desperate. **Effect** says how much you accomplish if it goes right: limited, standard, or great. A player can push for greater effect by accepting a worse position in return. The dice themselves are deliberately crude: a pool of six-sided dice from which you keep the single highest. The whole craft of the system lives in that pre-roll framing rather than in modifiers. [corpus]
:::

## Mechanisms illustrated

This is the cross-linked heart of the profile. Each rule below points to the article it exemplifies, so the gazetteer and the species accounts read as one fabric.

**Position and effect** is the clearest worked example of a graded outcome system in the guide. Instead of a target number, the roll's meaning is set by two narrative dials, which is the technique [Mechanical Resolution Types](../articles/systems-mechanics/mechanical-resolution-types.qmd) calls separating *how dangerous* from *how effective*. [corpus]

**Progress clocks** are segmented circles drawn on the table to track any building situation, such as a rising alarm or a wound that has begun to close. They sit in the open as a shared picture of pressure. A filled clock triggers a change. The mechanism is generic enough that it has spread far beyond this game, and [Progress Clocks & Faction State](../articles/systems-mechanics/progress-clocks-and-faction-state.qmd) treats it as the canonical tension tool. [corpus]

**The flashback** lets a player spend stress mid-job to establish a precaution their character took earlier, resolved as an ordinary roll. It inverts the planning problem that [Prep Economy](../articles/systems-mechanics/prep-economy.qmd) names. The crew never budgets time to plan before the job; the game skips that scene and charges for each precaution as it surfaces, which hands the table the heist-film rhythm that [Heist & Caper Patterns](../articles/story-character-voice/heist-and-caper-patterns.qmd) is built around. [corpus]

**The two playbook layers** are a strong case for [Playbook Design](../articles/systems-mechanics/playbook-design.qmd). Each character picks a playbook of action ratings and abilities, and the crew as a whole picks a second one that grants shared abilities and a home turf. Advancement runs on both tracks at once, so the gang develops as its own character. [corpus]

**Faction clocks** turn the setting into a world-engine. Doskvol's factions hold a tier and a standing toward the crew, and they advance their own agendas between sessions whether or not the players intervene, which is the autonomous-agenda problem at the core of [Faction Design & Asymmetric Objectives](../articles/players-social-dynamics/faction-design-and-asymmetric-objectives.qmd). [corpus] The crew's reputation gates what those factions will offer; its tier sets the scale of job it can attempt.

## The wider influence

In December 2017 Harper released the Forged in the Dark SRD under a Creative Commons CC-BY licence, keeping only the mechanics and explicitly inviting derivative games. [corpus] By 2023 itch.io listed more than three hundred products carrying the tag, from the space-opera *Scum and Villainy* to the magical-girl *Girl by Moonlight*. [corpus] The permissive licence, attribution without share-alike, did as much to grow that family as the rules did, and it pushed open licensing toward the norm in the indie scene. [synthesis]

The parts have also travelled on their own. Designers who have never read the game still use progress clocks, still frame rolls as position against effect, still structure a campaign as an alternation of high-pressure operations and quieter recovery. That portability is the real lesson of the profile. A rule that does one job cleanly, with no dependency on the rest of the system, becomes a tool the whole field can lift.

::: {.callout-warning}
### The Hazard

A clock borrowed without its pressure is just a tally. Blades's clocks bite because filling one *does something* the table already fears, and because position and effect keep raising the stakes that feed them. Lifting the circle onto a game that has no equivalent source of escalation gives you the picture of tension without the tension. [synthesis]
:::

## The stat block

| Attribute | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Medium | Tabletop roleplaying game |
| Designer | John Harper (One Seven Design) |
| Published | 2017, Evil Hat Productions |
| Players | A GM and one to four players sharing a single crew |
| Format | Campaign play, run as scores of roughly three to four hours |
| Substrate | Core rulebook, pools of six-sided dice, character and crew playbooks, segmented clocks |
| Open licence | Forged in the Dark SRD (Creative Commons CC-BY, 2017) |

## Sources

- Harper, John. *Blades in the Dark*. Evil Hat Productions, 2017. Core rules: <https://bladesinthedark.com/basics>
- Harper, John. "Forged in the Dark" SRD. One Seven Design, 2017. <https://bladesinthedark.com/forged-dark>
- "Blades in the Dark." Wikipedia. (Publication history, the position-and-effect system, progress clocks, the flashback, and the Forged in the Dark family.) <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Blades_in_the_Dark>
- "Blades in the Dark." Kickstarter, One Seven Design, 2015. (Funding history: one-day funding, final raise.) <https://www.kickstarter.com/projects/2080350433/blades-in-the-dark>
- "Blades in the Dark." RPG Museum. (Crew and character playbook structure, faction tiers and clocks.) <https://rpgmuseum.fandom.com/wiki/Blades_in_the_Dark>

<!-- BACKLINKS:START (generated by scripts/backlinks.py — do not edit by hand) -->

## Referenced by

- [Exemplars](index.qmd)

<!-- BACKLINKS:END -->
